content.problemStatement:
Chef wants to impress Chefina by giving her the maximum number of gifts possible.

Chef is in a gift shop having NNN items where the cost of the ithi^{th}ith item is equal to AiA_{i}Ai​.
Chef has KKK amount of money and a 50%50 \%50% off discount coupon that he can use for **at most one** of the items he buys.

If the cost of an item is equal to XXX, then, after applying the coupon on that item, Chef only has to pay ⌈X2⌉{\bf \left\lceil \frac{X}{2} \right\rceil}⌈2X​⌉ (rounded up to the nearest integer) amount for that item.

Help Chef find the **maximum number** of items he can buy with KKK amount of money and a 50%50 \%50% discount coupon given that he can use the coupon on **at most one** item.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers, where the ithi^{th}ith integer AiA_{i}Ai​ denotes the cost of the ithi^{th}ith item.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ― the **maximum number** of items Chef can buy.
constraints: 1 ≤ N ≤ 10^5 (sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two integers N and K. The next line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN, the costs of the items.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with one integer – the maximum number of items Chef can buy using at most one 50% discount coupon.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: Discounted cost of the only item is ceil(5/2)=3 ≤4, so Chef buys 1 item.\nTest case 2: Sorted costs are [4,4,5]; buying all three costs 4+4+5 - floor(5/2)=13-2=11 ≤15, so answer is 3.\nTest case 3: Sorted costs [4,6,7]; buying first two items costs 4+6 - floor(6/2)=10-3=7 ≤10, but buying three would need 17-3=14 >10, so maximum is 2.

solution.approach: For a fixed set of items the cheapest way to use the coupon is on the most expensive item among the chosen ones, because the discount (⌈X/2⌉) gives the largest saving for larger X. Therefore the optimal set of m items is the m cheapest items. Let the costs be sorted increasingly: a1 ≤ a2 ≤ … ≤ aN. Let pref[i] be the sum of the first i items. If we buy the first i items and apply the coupon to ai, the total money spent is pref[i] - floor(ai/2) (since ai is replaced by ceil(ai/2) = ai - floor(ai/2)). The minimum money needed for i items is exactly this value. We iterate i from 1 to N and keep the largest i such that pref[i] - floor(ai/2) ≤ K. The answer is that i (or 0 if none satisfy). The algorithm runs in O(N log N) per test case because of sorting, and O(N) additional memory for the prefix sums.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (sorting dominates).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the array and prefix sums.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it)); K = int(next(it))
        a = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        a.sort()
        pref = 0
        ans = 0
        for i, val in enumerate(a, 1):
            pref += val
            cost = pref - val // 2  # floor division
            if cost <= K:
                ans = i
        out_lines.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
